next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

At Marsh Crescent the outage took the fans first, then the strip lights, then the little courtesies that made custody look orderly.

The emergency bars along the corridor floor glowed a bad hospital green. Everything above them sweated in the dark. White wall panels that looked clean by daylight showed their seams at night, rust pushing through the paint in hairline blooms. The train had been standing still for almost an hour on the marshland siding, nose angled into black water and reeds, with the traction line dead and the backup system doing just enough to keep locks live and nothing else. There was no refrigeration in the galley, no proper air, and no false sense that movement itself was mercy.

Heat sat inside the cars like a second load. It pressed Marisol's shirt flat against her back and worked salt into the chemical burn scar on her wrist until the skin there prickled. Mosquitoes worried at the torn patch of screen over the crew vestibule window, whining so close to her ear that more than once she slapped at nothing and hit the metal wall. Beneath her boots the floor no longer carried the steady travel hum of the line. Instead it gave her the slow settling sounds of stalled tanks and strained couplings: a hollow shift in the blackwater below, a tinny pop from cooling bolts, somewhere farther down a latch knocking softly every time a body leaned against the wrong door.

She sat on an overturned waste drum in the service alcove with a flashlight, a dead comm tablet, and Gabriel's recorder laid across her thigh. The little machine looked cheap enough to h...
open_hooks_to_carry: Use sanitation procedure itself as the means of delay.
Show the practical inconvenience and moral ambiguity of the hold for workers and detainees alike.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...r leaving some domestic task half done. Marisol signed her name so hard the stylus skipped.

"Everything fine?" he asked, routine more than curiosity.

"No," she said.

He looked at her, uncertain whether that required action. She was already walking away before he could decide.

The dispatch corridor seemed narrower than it had an hour earlier. Freighted carts clogged one side, water drums the other. A deep-clean rig waited near sanitation stores with its tamper seals still bright red, the bleach canisters strapped tight, the clipboard fat with forms that could stop a route cold if any one box on them turned from preventative to active. Beyond it, through the glass, the departure board counted down green minutes toward southbound release.

Marisol stood with the names damp against her wrist and listened to the building. Pumps. Vents. Wheel checks. The two-language politeness of the announcements. Teresa had been right about one thing. The file was not a biography. It would not open for grief, or blood, or the fact that Tom Avelar had become more useful dead than he had ever managed alive. Law had shut every door that used its own name.

But the train still belonged to pressure, to waste, to inventory, to all the ugly practical nouns the company feared because they cost money and left residue. Marisol knew those nouns better than any lawyer ever would. She knew what had to be scrubbed, sealed, vented, carried, and kept from overflowing. She knew how close the whole corridor lived to stoppage while pretending it lived on order.

The southbound clock kept moving.

So did she.
state_deltas_required: Dispatch clocks, audit trails, and the first material lie Marisol asks others to carry.
